title: Orecchiette con cime di rapa
subtitle: Broccoli rabe, anchovy, and garlic over Pugliese ear pasta
excerpt: Orecchiette with bitter broccoli rabe, anchovy, garlic, and chilli — the defining pasta of Bari and the Puglia coast. The broccoli rabe cooks in the pasta water and is tossed through the pasta with anchovy sauce that fades into the background as seasoning. Bitter, sharp, and deeply satisfying.
sourceNotes: Orecchiette con le cime di rapa is the signature dish of Bari, in Puglia, where the pasta shape and the preparation are both documented from the sixteenth century. The dish is included in regional surveys of Pugliese cooking dating to the late nineteenth century. Artusi's discussion of semolina pasta and greens in La scienza in cucina (1891, public domain) covers the broader family. Cime di rapa (turnip tops or broccoli rabe) can be substituted with purple sprouting broccoli, which has a similar bitterness.

content.text: The brilliance of this dish is that the broccoli rabe cooks in the same water as the pasta. Start the greens earlier; they need longer. Add the pasta to the same pot once the greens are mostly tender. When both are done, drain together and finish in the anchovy pan. The starchy, green-tinged pasta water goes into the sauce.

Purple sprouting broccoli works if you cannot find cime di rapa. Tenderstem broccoli is milder and less bitter, but still produces a good result. The anchovy dissolves completely into the oil and garlic — you do not taste anchovy, you taste depth and salt. Anyone who says they dislike anchovies should be given this without being told.

items.prepNote: or cime di rapa; tough stalks trimmed, roughly chopped

content.text: Cook the greens and pasta together

Bring a large pan of generously salted water to a boil. Add the {{purple-sprouting-broccoli}} of broccoli rabe and cook for 5 minutes. Add the {{pasta-dried}} of orecchiette to the same pot. Cook for the time on the packet minus 2 minutes. Scoop out 200 ml of the pasta water before draining. Drain together.

While the pasta cooks, warm the {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}} of olive oil in a wide frying pan over medium heat. Add the {{garlic}} of garlic and the anchovies. Cook for 2 minutes, pressing the anchovies with a spoon until they dissolve into the oil. Add the chilli flakes. The oil should be fragrant and the garlic pale gold.

Add the drained pasta and broccoli to the anchovy pan over medium heat. Add 100 ml of the pasta water. Toss vigorously for 2 minutes until the sauce coats everything. Add more pasta water if needed — the dish should be moist, not dry. Taste for seasoning; the anchovy is salty, so check before adding any salt. Serve immediately with a drizzle of extra-virgin olive oil.

items.symptom: Broccoli is too soft and mushy
items.cause: Added at the same time as the pasta; the greens need a head start
items.fix: Add the broccoli rabe 5 minutes before the pasta goes in

items.symptom: Dish tastes of anchovy rather than seasoning
items.cause: Too many anchovies, or they were not fully dissolved into the oil
items.fix: Press the anchovies in the hot oil until they completely disappear; use only 6 fillets for 4 portions

items.cause: Undercooked, or not enough pasta water in the finish
items.fix: Add pasta water generously; the starch loosens the pasta and the sauce

items.cause: Cime di rapa or PSB not blanched long enough to reduce bitterness
items.fix: Cook the greens for the full 5 minutes in the pasta water before adding the pasta

content.text: Orecchiette con le cime di rapa is the dish Bari serves to strangers as proof of what Pugliese cooking can do with very little. The pasta shape (small ear-shaped rounds, pressed with the thumb against a wooden board) is made by hand in every household in the city, and sold fresh from the windows of houses along Bari's old town. The combination of bitter greens, anchovy, and garlic is ancient: it requires no cheese, no meat, no expensive ingredient. The poverty of the Mezzogiorno produced some of Italy's most intelligent food, and this is among its best examples.
